ppt.title: Science: Acids, Bases and Salts - Session 1
ppt.presentationGoal: To introduce the fundamental properties of acids and bases through sensory experiences, natural indicators, and chemical definitions.

licensingNotes: Use Wikimedia Commons images by default and use in-app SVG/native slide text only as a fallback.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Use SVG diagrams if specific Wikimedia assets are unavailable.

licenseChecklist: Verify all image sources allow commercial/educational reuse.
Ensure attribution text is accurate and complete.
presentationWarnings: Do not taste or touch unknown chemicals in the classroom demonstration section without safety gear.
Use dilute solutions for student activities to ensure safety.

learningOutcomesCovered: Identify acids and bases using sensory properties
Distinguish between natural, synthetic, and olfactory indicators
Explain the reaction of acids/bases with litmus paper
Define Arrhenius concept of H+ and OH- ions
topicsCovered: Sensory properties of acids and bases
taughtConceptsCovered: Acidic vs Basic nature based on taste and touch

slides.teachingBeat: Set the stage for the chapter and establish safety protocols.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Acids, Bases and Salts: Session 1
> Acids, Bases and Salts
> Introduction to Chemical Nature
- Grade 10 Science
- Chapter 2: Acids, Bases and Salts
- Session 1 of 6
![Acids, Bases and Salts: Session 1](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/4dc62428e9b5c52590f296371552e129a7ec76d4.svg)

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: title_identity -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Set the stage for the chapter and establish safety protocols.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter topic.
Explain: Today we begin our journey into acids and bases. We will start by observing their properties before diving into complex chemistry.
Ask: Who has tasted lemon juice? What about soap?
Listen for: Descriptions of sour taste or slippery feel.
Board work: Write 'Chapter 2' on the board with today's date.
Transition: Let's start by recalling what we know from everyday life.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Set the stage for the chapter and establish safety protocols.
slides.studentTakeaway: Understand that acids and bases are distinct chemical families with unique properties.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Set the stage for the chapter and establish safety protocols.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter topic.
Explain: Today we begin our journey into acids and bases. We will start by observing their properties before diving into complex chemistry.
Ask: Who has tasted lemon juice? What about soap?
Listen for: Descriptions of sour taste or slippery feel.
Board work: Write 'Chapter 2' on the board with today's date.
Transition: Let's start by recalling what we know from everyday life.

slides.teachingBeat: Connect abstract chemical concepts to tangible student experiences.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Acids in Our Daily Lives
> How do we identify them?
> - Taste (Caution!)
- - Touch
- - Color Change Indicators
- Sour taste: Lemon, Vinegar, Curd
![Acids in Our Daily Lives](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/7c7d2a34729b85d7126964e5f86c7f23cf95aee6.svg)

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: lesson_hook -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Connect abstract chemical concepts to tangible student experiences.
Purpose: Hook students with relatable examples.
Explain: Acids are often sour. Bases can be bitter and feel soapy or slippery to touch.
Ask: Why do we not taste chemicals in the lab?
Listen for: Safety reasons, toxicity concerns.
Correct: Emphasize that tasting unknown substances is dangerous; scientists use indicators instead.
Board work: List 'Sour' under Acids and 'Bitter/Slippery' under Bases on the board.
-->
topicCoverage: Sensory properties of acids and bases
slides.teacherIntent: Connect abstract chemical concepts to tangible student experiences.
slides.studentTakeaway: Recognize that acids and bases are present in common household items.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Connect abstract chemical concepts to tangible student experiences.
Purpose: Hook students with relatable examples.
Explain: Acids are often sour. Bases can be bitter and feel soapy or slippery to touch.
Ask: Why do we not taste chemicals in the lab?
Listen for: Safety reasons, toxicity concerns.
Correct: Emphasize that tasting unknown substances is dangerous; scientists use indicators instead.
Board work: List 'Sour' under Acids and 'Bitter/Slippery' under Bases on the board.
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Acids in Our Daily Lives that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains lesson_hook.

slides.teachingBeat: Introduce the most common indicator and its color changes.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Litmus Paper Test
> Litmus Paper Behavior:
> - Acid (+) : Turns RED
- Source: Extracted from lichens
- Acidic Solution -> Red Color
- Basic Solution -> Blue Color
![Litmus Paper Test](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/371e5853bda4f268803fdb01ff02834c0900d077.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Introduce the most common indicator and its color changes.
Purpose: Explain the mechanism of litmus paper.
Explain: Litmus is a natural dye obtained from lichens. It acts as a pH indicator.
Ask: What happens if we dip blue litmus in acid?
Listen for: Turns red.
Correct: Yes, it turns red. If we use red litmus on an acid? No change (remains red).
Board work: Draw a simple flowchart: Acid -> Red | Base -> Blue.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the most common indicator and its color changes.
slides.studentTakeaway: Litmus turns red in acids and blue in bases; purple in neutral solutions.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Introduce the most common indicator and its color changes.
Purpose: Explain the mechanism of litmus paper.
Explain: Litmus is a natural dye obtained from lichens. It acts as a pH indicator.
Ask: What happens if we dip blue litmus in acid?
Listen for: Turns red.
Correct: Yes, it turns red. If we use red litmus on an acid? No change (remains red).
Board work: Draw a simple flowchart: Acid -> Red | Base -> Blue.
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Litmus Paper Test that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains core_concept.

slides.teachingBeat: Demonstrate a unique color change specific to bases.
mustTeach: No change with Acids or Neutral
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Turmeric: A Yellow to Red Change
> Turmeric Indicator:
> - Yellow (Neutral/Acid)
- Natural indicator from turmeric root
- Yellow color initially
- Turns Reddish-Brown with Bases
![Turmeric: A Yellow to Red Change](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/fbf49c73caa5ed3a0aec8e5f9ace944c178834fb.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: visual_explainer -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Demonstrate a unique color change specific to bases.
Purpose: Highlight the specific reaction of turmeric.
Explain: Turmeric is a spice that acts as an indicator. It changes color only in basic conditions.
Ask: If we add soap solution to turmeric paste, what happens?
Listen for: Turns red/brown.
Correct: Yes! This confirms the presence of a base (soap).
Board work: Write 'Turmeric + Base = Reddish Brown'.
-->
slides.slideTitle: Turmeric: A Yellow to Red Change
slides.teacherIntent: Demonstrate a unique color change specific to bases.
slides.studentTakeaway: Turmeric turns from yellow to reddish-brown in basic solutions but stays yellow in acids and neutrals.
bulletPoints: No change with Acids or Neutral
speakerNotes: Purpose: Demonstrate a unique color change specific to bases.
Purpose: Highlight the specific reaction of turmeric.
Explain: Turmeric is a spice that acts as an indicator. It changes color only in basic conditions.
Ask: If we add soap solution to turmeric paste, what happens?
Listen for: Turns red/brown.
Correct: Yes! This confirms the presence of a base (soap).
Board work: Write 'Turmeric + Base = Reddish Brown'.
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Turmeric: A Yellow to Red Change that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains visual_explainer.

slides.teachingBeat: Introduce the concept of olfactory indicators for visually impaired students.
mustTeach: - Onion extract loses smell in base
- Vanilla essence loses smell in base
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Smell-Based Indicators
> Smell Changes:
> - Acidic Solution : No change (usually)
- Olfactory Indicators: Change odor
- - Onion extract loses smell in base
- - Vanilla essence loses smell in base
![Smell-Based Indicators](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/3a396058a5253a574a0b7e8a739b47e37fd15a40.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Introduce the concept of olfactory indicators for visually impaired students.
Purpose: Explain olfactory indicators.
Explain: These are substances whose smell changes in acidic or basic media. This is crucial for inclusive education.
Ask: What happens to vanilla essence when added to a base?
Listen for: The smell goes away.
Correct: Yes, the odor disappears in bases but remains in acids.
Board work: List 'Onion' and 'Vanilla' as examples of olfactory indicators.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the concept of olfactory indicators for visually impaired students.
slides.studentTakeaway: Some substances change their smell in acidic or basic solutions (e.g., onion, vanilla).
bulletPoints: - Onion extract loses smell in base
- Vanilla essence loses smell in base
speakerNotes: Purpose: Introduce the concept of olfactory indicators for visually impaired students.
Purpose: Explain olfactory indicators.
Explain: These are substances whose smell changes in acidic or basic media. This is crucial for inclusive education.
Ask: What happens to vanilla essence when added to a base?
Listen for: The smell goes away.
Correct: Yes, the odor disappears in bases but remains in acids.
Board work: List 'Onion' and 'Vanilla' as examples of olfactory indicators.
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Smell-Based Indicators that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains core_concept.

slides.teachingBeat: Explain the chemical basis of acidity (H+) and basicity (OH-) through ionization.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Why Do They Conduct Electricity?
> Acid Dissociation:
> HA -> H+ + A-
- BOH -> B+ + OH-
- Dissolving in Water -> Ion Formation
- - Acid: Releases Hydrogen Ions (H+)
![Why Do They Conduct Electricity?](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/558b98f6b01ca32211c6c210b0bd4110aad569c0.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Explain the chemical basis of acidity (H+) and basicity (OH-) through ionization.
Purpose: Introduce the Arrhenius concept.
Explain: When acids dissolve in water, they split into ions. The H+ ion is responsible for acidic properties.
Ask: Why does pure distilled water not conduct electricity?
Listen for: No ions present.
Correct: Exactly! Tap water conducts because of dissolved salts/ions.
Board work: Write the dissociation equations clearly on the board. Highlight H+ and OH- in different colors.
-->
topicCoverage: Ionization in water: H+ and OH- ions
slides.teacherIntent: Explain the chemical basis of acidity (H+) and basicity (OH-) through ionization.
slides.studentTakeaway: Acids release H+ ions; Bases release OH- ions when dissolved in water. This is why they conduct electricity.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Explain the chemical basis of acidity (H+) and basicity (OH-) through ionization.
Purpose: Introduce the Arrhenius concept.
Explain: When acids dissolve in water, they split into ions. The H+ ion is responsible for acidic properties.
Ask: Why does pure distilled water not conduct electricity?
Listen for: No ions present.
Correct: Exactly! Tap water conducts because of dissolved salts/ions.
Board work: Write the dissociation equations clearly on the board. Highlight H+ and OH- in different colors.
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Why Do They Conduct Electricity? that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains core_concept.

svgDiagram.title: Ionization Process
instructions: Show HCl molecule splitting into H+ and Cl- ions in water.
Show NaOH molecule splitting into Na+ and OH- ions.
svgDiagram.svgCode: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 400 200"><rect width="400" height="200" fill="#f9f9f9"/><text x="50" y="50" font-family="Arial" font-size="16">HCl + H₂O → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻</text><circle cx="80" cy="70" r="10" fill="#e74c3c"/><text x="95" y="75" font-family="Arial" font-size="12">H⁺</text><rect x="60" y="60" width="20" height="20" stroke="#333" fill="none"/><text x="50" y="90" font-family="Arial" font-size="14">Acidic Solution (H⁺)</text><line x1="180" y1="70" x2="220" y2="70" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M 225,65 L 240,70 L 225,75 Z" fill="#333"/><text x="180" y="90" font-family="Arial" font-size="14">Basic Solution (OH⁻)</text><rect x="240" y="60" width="20" height="20" stroke="#e74c3c" fill="none"/><circle cx="250" cy="70" r="8" fill="#3498db"/><text x="260" y="75" font-family="Arial" font-size="12">OH⁻</text></svg>

- Bases: Bitter/Slippery, OH- ions, Blue Litmus
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Summary: Acids and Bases
> Key Takeaways:
> 1. Sensory Properties
- 2. Indicator Reactions
- 3. Ion Formation (H+/OH-)
- - Acids: Sour, H+ ions, Red Litmus
![Summary: Acids and Bases](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/a4e78418caa936f1fa18b7257e048ceb924862f7.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: summary -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Consolidate learning points and prepare for the next session.
Purpose: Wrap up the session.
Explain: We have learned how to identify acids and bases using our senses and indicators, and why they conduct electricity due to ions.
Ask: Which indicator would you use if you couldn't see?
Listen for: Olfactory indicators like onion or vanilla.
Transition: Tomorrow we will explore the pH scale which quantifies this acidity.
Board work: Write 'Homework: List 5 household items that are acidic and 5 that are basic.'.
-->

slides.studentTakeaway: Acids taste sour, bases feel soapy; indicators help identify them chemically via color or smell changes due to ion formation.
bulletPoints: - Acids: Sour, H+ ions, Red Litmus
